title: Chapter 2. A Shelf That Wasn't There Yesterday
content:
The south alcove was supposed to end with a blank wall of green marble. Elian knew the proportions by memory. Seven shelves on the left, seven on the right, the wall ahead, and the narrow reading niche beneath the lamp.

Tonight there were eight shelves on the left.

She did not approach immediately. It was an old rule of hers, one never written in the archive manuals: when a room changes, let the room admit it first.

The lamp in the niche burned with its usual pearled light. Nothing seemed wrong except the extra unit of walnut shelving, fitted so neatly between two original uprights that the eye wanted to forgive it.

Elian crossed the stone tiles one measured step at a time. The new shelf held twelve books, all unmarked at the spine. Their bindings were matte black, like paper rubbed smooth by handling, but when she touched one it felt faintly warm.

Not warm from a fire. Warm like a hand had just left it.

The first page of the first book contained a single line.

If a memory is cataloged, does it stop wandering?

Elian shut the cover at once, more annoyed than afraid. The archive had trained her out of theatrical responses. But annoyance was sometimes only fear with better posture.

She took the book to the reading niche, set it beside the lamp, and realized that the unsigned note in the ledger had not warned her about an alcove becoming longer. It had warned her about the hour in which it would happen.
